Hijack Credibility

can write a bad email and still make good sales. Basically what I'm talking about is
combing through your testimonials and finding the ones from people with "celebrity
appeal" and then building emails around them. Here are a few examples:

Secret Of The Sublist
Over the past couple years I've done a LOT of launches using emails. Mosdy, they
are for my own products, but I've also helped others do the same. And one of the big
"keys" (as you'll see next month) is having a sublist. A sublist is a list of people who are on
your main list, who have raised their hands and said, "yes, I am. interested in
knowing 111ore about this product when it launches!" There are many reasons
for creating a sublist the second you know you're going to launch a new product (even
months in advance). But one of the main reasons is to simply monitor demand. You can
see, at a glance, how many people are even interested in your product. If nobody joins it,
then you may want to go back to the drawing board and rethink launching that product.
On the other hand, if you get a bunch of people signing up, then you're probably going
to have a successful launch. No, it doesn't mean they will ALL buy. But I look at it sort of
like an RSVP. These are people who have gotten back to me (via opting in to the sublist)
and said they're interested in attending the sale (as buyers). Next month I'll talk about this
more. But for now, if you are creating a new product... I implore you to put up a squeeze
page and sending your list to it -- today. Tell them if they want your new product at a
discount (or whatever incentive you want to give for people to buy during the launch) to
enter their email address yada yada yada. Anyway, more on this next month ...
Make The Skeleton Dance
One of my "big secrets" of writing emails is (oddly enough) not studying other
email marketers or copywriters... but instead studying top sales professionals. The kind of
people who sell face-to-face or over the phone -- and not via a computer or the Internet
(they might still use the Internet to sell, but they close the deal on the phone or in person).
And one person I've been studying like crazy lately is this dude Barry Maher. Selling
Power Magazine called Barry, " ... simply the best sales trainer in the business." And his
client lists range from ABC, Capital Cities and the American Management Association to
Fuji Film and the National Lottery of Ireland to Verizon, not to mention Ameritech, Bell
South and SBC. He's been featured in USA Today, the New York Times, Business Week,
Success, the "Vall Streetjournal. In other words the bloke's no dummy. Anyway, he
teaches lots of great info that can be directly applied to emails. But by FAR the most
powerful is what he calls "bragging about the negative." Yes, most of us know the power
of admitting flaws in our products and services. But how many people BRAG about
tl1em? Not too many, methinks. But there's a boatload of money in "making the skeleton
dance" like this. Here's how Barry put it in an interview with Michael SenofT:

Michael: In the first chapter of your book, you talk about
m.aking the skeleton dance. What does that mean?
Barry: Well, it comes from a quote from George Bernard Shaw.
If you can't get rid of the family skeleton, you might as well
make it dance. Now, we've all got negatives in every thing we're
presenting against the negative idea. So, what can you do? You
can hide them. You can basically lie to the customer, hide them.
You can mealy mouse them and try to slip them by and not
notice, or you can bring them out and brag about them. It's a lot
easier to bring them out and brag about them just like I did a
few minute ago when I was talking about my fees. Are my fees
expensive? Absolutely. Why do I charge so much? Because I can
-because my clients aren't just willing to pay these kinds of
rates, they're happy to pay them for the kind of results I
generate. So, I'm taking a negative which is this huge negative
we all think about-- price, "Oh my gosh, price", and turning it
into a positive. Somebody asks me about my price, "Yeah,
they're expensive. I'm thinking of raising them."
This, my fine feathered friend, is pureJEENIUS. And I've been doing this more
and more in emails (not just to my BenSettle.com list but other lists I write emails to) and
ads. It works and works and works ... most people (including your competition!) are trying
to hide the family skeleton. Or, at the very least, dressing him up in a bulky overcoat and a
hat pulled down its face to hide its honey grin ... to give the illusion it's not a skeleton but a
living body. .. and it just makes people more skeptical and less likely to buy. So instead,
take your product's negatives ... its drawbacks ... and don'tjust admit them, BRAG about
them. Make that skeleton salsa dance, baby! Incidentally, Barry talks more about this in
the above interview which I have enclosed with this issue (see the golden rod bonus report
-- I've listened to this interview dozens of times and always get something new out of it
for emails each time ... I bet you will too.)

The $5 Milkshake Secret
One of my favorite movies is "Pulp Fiction." Don't ask me why-- but I can watch
it over and over and over (in the same day) and it never gets boring. But anyhoo, there's a
very cool marketing lesson in it for us email marketers. I call it "The $5 milkshake secret."
If you've seen the movie, Vincent takes Mia to dinner and she orders a milkshake that's
literally called "The $5 Milkshakc." Vince is visibly surprised. How does a shake-- milk
and ice cream - cost $5? Bottom line... he HAD to taste it. (Wouldn't you?) And guess
what? Slapping a high price on a quality product/service is one of the single BEST ways
to create curiosity you can ever use. I'm not saying to gouge anyone. But for some reason
people think they can win on price. You can't. At least, not on cutting price. But you can
win on RAISING price. Claude Hopkins even wrote about this in his copywriting book.
You can also see entire media events around things like "the $50 hamburger." Big price
gets big attention and curiosit}'z Test it and methinks you'll be pleasandy surprised. And it
could be as simple as this template (feel free to play around with it and change it however
you see fit -- it's "copyright free"):

if price is the most important thing than you should choose them.
But 	is 	for people who don't want to waste time.
Who don't want a __ with problems later.

An Email Lesson From
The World's Greatest Telephone Salesn1an
I mentioned earlier how I'm big on studying phone and belly-to-belly salesmen for
email marketing. And the "best of the best" telephone salesman is Stan Billue. Stan is the
guy whose training materials other popular sales trainers (like Tom Hopkins and Zig
Ziglar) use when selling seats to their own seminars. Anyway, one of the single most
powerful EMAIL secrets I ever heard came from him. This story is so good1 it's worth an
entire week of thinking about and implementing right away in your NEXT email.
Anyway, he once told the story about when he was selling high ticket investments and he
called a doctor who was so angry and skeptical he hung up on Stan as soon as he heard
the word "investment." So Stan calls him right back and says, "I'm sorry, I think we got
disconnected... " and continued on (an old school telephone technique). But the good
doctor stopped him in his tracks again, "no you litde S.O.B. we didn't get disconnected, I
hung up on you and if you ever call back I'm going to report you to blah blah blah ... "
and slammed the phone down again. Long story short, Stan called back a few more times
only to get a busy signal. But about a half hour later, Stan got through again. The doctor
angrily picked up the phone, "Yeah!!?"
"Doc, before you hang up on me a third time I have to ask you one more thing!"
"What?"
"You must have had a HORRIBLE experience with investment salesmen... "

And from there, the guy talked and talked and talked ... until, after 15 minutes, a
sale for over $200k was made. Okay, there's a lot more to this story and in a future "Email
Players" issue I'll tell you more about it. There are more details I'm leaving out on how he
closed the sale (it's a truly fascinating story, too, as after that Stan SOUGHT OUT angry
prospects, and found they are the easiest to sell with his methods). But for now the take
away is this one single concept that, if you learn it, take it to heart and USE it, you'll
never have trouble getting your emails opened and read ever again. As Stan says...
"Always remember... curiosity always overpowers programming. He was
progra.nuned to blow me off the phone again but he couldn't get the words
out of his mouth." In other words ... when he called the angry guy back and simply
said, "I have to ask you one more thing!" the immediate reaction was, "What?" -- where
Stan now had complete control of the call. Yes. curiosity gives you control. With email
this is even MORE important. Everyone is programmed to delete emails on sight, right?
Especially emails that look like marketing. But if you embed curiosity into your emails --
in the subject lines, opening sentences, body copy and your offers... you almost cannot
lose. For examples, just look at most of my emails. I do this as often as possible. And
throughout future "Email Players" issues we'll be driving this point home a lot (with
examples). But for now your mission (if you choose to accept it) is to take the 7 lessons in
this newsletter and APPLY them to your emails --this week. There's a lot of$$ to he
made online, and these 7 tips can divert a good chunk of it into your pocket.
The "Meet The Master" Email
Well crap... I said we'd cover 7 ways but I can't help but squeeze an 8th one in
(hope you don't mind ... ). This is great for a first email. You don't have to do this, but
many times if you have an auto-responder sequence it can be a good # 1. Anyway, what it
is, is an email where you tell your story of woe and/ or show your credentials and how you
overcame the challenges of whatever it is you do ... and now you're going to show them
what you know in your emails. This lets people get to know you a bit, keeps things fun &
interesting, and inspires them to want to conquer the problem you help solve since they
saw you did it. This should give your future emails more impact. Here's an example from
an email I wrote for a client a little while back (I removed the name and URL for privacy

"Email Players" Bonus Report:
Barry Maher Interview Transcript
Here's an interview Michael Senoff did with a gentleman named Barry Maher. Barry first made his mark
as a world class salesperson. Then, as a manager and sales consultant helping improve clients
productivity. Selling Power Magazine declared Barry, " ... to his powerful and famous client, Barry Maber
is simply the best sales trainer in the business." His client lists range from ABC, Capital Cities and the
American Management Association to Fuji Film and the National Lottery of Ireland to Verizon, not to
mention Ameritecb, Bell South and SBC. He's been featured in USA Today, the New York Times,
Business Week, Success, the Wall Street Journal. The reason I am lncludioe It ip this month 's
pewsletter js because there are a Jot of prjpciples be teaches that are directly applicable to writipg
emails that sell like craey (i.e. making the skeleton dance, bmgging about the negative, etc)
Enjoy!

Email Player Jules Oancy (www.TheStoneSoup.com) recently asked some great
questions a lot of subscribers call benefit from knowing the answers to. They have to
do with hard teaching vs soft teaching and how to pick which products to sell on
which days.
We'll be going over this in more depth in future "Email Players" issues.
But for now, here's some "food" for thought (yeah, pun intended, sue me).
My answers are in bold below:
# 	# 	#
Hey Ben
Thanks for the feedback on my last email.. tried out your idea and picked up a few
extra sales so you're earning your keep :)
Couple of questions...
1. Really intrigued by the concept of soft teaching v's hard teaching. I can see how it
works well for you but struggling to get it to fit to my niche. Should I be talking about
the benefits of particular foods I techniques (ie the why someone should do
something) and then 	directing them to my books I classes rather than giving the
recipe? At the moment I'm pretty much always including a recipe in my emails. How
would you approach the soft teaching for a market that's used to free recipes?
I think if your list is big enough (say, 4k or more, or even Sk) you should test
including a recipe with each email vs not doing it. But normally, that sort of thing is
likely to hurt sales. (Not always though). I like to think of it as someone who gives
away samples in the grocery store. The lady comes up to you with a sample piece of
cheese. You really like it and it kind of makes you more hungry, so you go and buy a
whole block of cheese or whatever. But if he gives you a whole pound of cheese and
you're no longer hungry, your chances of buying are drastically lower because now
you're full. This whole analogy is kind of apt for your product, but people are more
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 31 of 33 --

likely to buy food when they're "hungry" than if they're already satisfied, so our job
is to make fan the flames of that hunger .
So yeah, if I were you I'd talk about everything BUT the actual recipes.
Or maybe just zero in on a specific ingredient and use that as an excuse to plug your
product ("and you can learn how to use this ingredient in over a dozen recipes at ...
link ")
Or just talk about food!
Pretend you 're hosting a talk radio show just about food.
You probably are not going to give out entire recipes, but you might give people
pointers on how to better prepare food, where to get the best ingredients, what to
avoid, what mistakes people make, etc. Then it's very easy to segue from those things
into buying your product . You can also talk about the frustrations they have (that's
huge!)
You're always safe in their world- talking about their pains, desires, frustrations, etc.
So talk about food/ingredients/benefits (like in the David Chang email you sent to be
critiqued) but they have to buy your product to learn how to prepare the meals and
you always want to imply your methods are unique in some way.
2. Plugging multiple products to the one list
I like how your 	bensettle.com list is continually plugging the one product with a
clear message in every email. My problem is I h ave heaps of products from classes to
ebooks to print books and I want to be able to promote different products at different
times. If you h ad different products , how would you manage them? Create different
lists? Or focus on one product for a few weeks then change to another? Or mix it up
on a daily basis?
This is a good question- there really is no one-size-fits all .
My preference is to do sequential selling -- sell the opt in (and nothing else) and then
my flagship product (and nothing else), and then after that people can buy anything
else I sell or affiliate offers, etc. This is in line with human nature and the way we are
used to buying.
But, some people do very well selling a mish-mash of products .
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 32 of 33 --

Matt Furey, for example, has a whole slew of products to choose from and he
alternates, nothing wrong with that at all. You could also do like you said and create
different lists, segmenting people by interest, that works too.
It's really up to you and what fits your style and preference.
I'm keen to learn more on the soft v's hard teaching... any recommended reading?
Here's The simplest way to think about it is, you want to tell people WHAT to do, but
now HOW to do it.
Example:
You can tell them what ingredients are awesome and why they're so great, and the
benefits, and the history of them (if they are interesting, of course).
But don't tell them how to use those ingredients in a specific recipe.
Hard teaching is giving a recipe, soft teaching is talking about how cool an ingredient
is how great it tastes, etc, but not telling them exactly how to use it in a recipe. Hard
teaching is "here's how to bake a pie do this and this and this" soft teaching is "here's
what people do wrong when baking pies, to learn the right way to do it check out my
book. .. " Hard teaching is telling them how to use the David Chang ingredient in a
recipe, soft teaching is telling them about how cool that ingredient it is and here's
where you can learn how I use it...
Just takes a bit of practice.
But eventually it becomes almost second nature when you write an email.
